did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a few-shot learning approach employing Siamese networks with ResNet-18 encoders to classify melanoma stages (based on lesion thickness) from dermoscopic images. The model achieved 77% accuracy on a limited dataset and 88% on an augmented dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a few-shot learning model using Siamese networks with ResNet encoders for melanoma stage classification. It includes specific sections on methodology (data preprocessing, model architecture), results (accuracy metrics, classification reports), and discussion of data leakage, indicating it is an original research study rather than a review.

title: Few-Shot Melanoma Stage Classification with Siamese Networks and ResNet Encoders: A Focus on Data Leakage Prevention
journal: 9th International Conference on Information Technology Research (ICITR)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma stage classification based on lesion thickness using a few-shot learning approach with Siamese networks and ResNet encoders. The model was trained on a curated dataset of dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive to handle data scarcity. To prevent data leakage, the researchers implemented rigorous data handling practices, ensuring independence between training and validation sets and avoiding standard data augmentation. The model achieved an accuracy of 77% on the limited dataset, while a version trained with ImageDataGenerator augmentation reached 88% but showed inconsistent metrics for minority classes. The results demonstrate the viability of Siamese networks for melanoma staging with limited data, provided that data leakage is strictly avoided.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on classifying melanoma stages based on lesion thickness using dermoscopic images. The specific target classes are Stage I (thin, < 0.76 mm), Stage II (intermediate, 0.76 mm - 1.5 mm), and Stage III (thick, > 1.5 mm). The dataset was curated from the ISIC Archive.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma stage (Stage I, Stage II, Stage III) based on lesion thickness
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of melanoma stages (Stage I: < 0.76 mm, Stage II: 0.76-1.5 mm, Stage III: > 1.5 mm) using dermoscopic images. The model uses a Siamese network with ResNet-18 encoders and categorical cross-entropy loss to predict discrete diagnostic categories. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for each stage.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive dataset of dermoscopic images curated from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive, containing images with melanoma and their corresponding stage labels based on lesion thickness.

use.training: Used for training the Siamese network model. The dataset was balanced by standardizing the number of images per stage to 40 to prevent data leakage and bias.
use.other: The paper mentions a version of the model trained on an augmented dataset using ImageDataGenerator, but does not specify if this used a separate test set or just the training data with augmentation. The primary evaluation was on the limited dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with a focus on comparing results to highlight the impact of data leakage rather than statistical significance.

ISIC Archive (Limited Dataset - No Augme.notes: The model achieved 77.42% accuracy on the limited dataset without data augmentation. The study highlights that this result is promising given the small dataset size and challenges of melanoma stage classification. The model demonstrated good performance in distinguishing between different stages, with the majority of predictions aligning with true labels.

ISIC Archive (Augmented Dataset - With I.experiment: Siamese Network with ResNet-18 Encoder (Trained on Augmented Data)
ISIC Archive (Augmented Dataset - With I.notes: The model trained with ImageDataGenerator augmentation achieved 88% accuracy. However, the paper notes inconsistencies in evaluation metrics, particularly for minority classes (Stage II), raising concerns about data leakage and inflated performance estimates. The support distribution changed significantly (Stage I: 44, Stage II: 6, Stage III: 10) compared to the limited dataset, suggesting potential data handling differences or leakage.

architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-18 encoder pre-trained on ImageNet dataset (over 1,000 classes)

architecture_details.backbone: ResNet-18 (CNN with 18 layers and residual connections)
architecture_details.structure: Two identical ResNet-18 encoders processing pairs of input images to generate feature vectors
architecture_details.comparison_method: Feature vectors compared using similarity metrics (Euclidean distance or cosine similarity)
architecture_details.feature_processing: Feature vectors concatenated and passed through fully connected layers

training_details.data_split: 90/5/5 split for training, validation, and testing
training_details.dataset_balancing: Standardized to 40 images per stage to prevent data leakage

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layers followed by Softmax activation
classification_or_output_head.function: Predicts probability distribution across melanoma stages

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing and Standardization: Images resized to 512x512 pixels for consistency.
Image Normalization: Pixel values normalized to a standard range to ensure comparability and enhance interpretability.
Denoising: Application of Super-Resolution Generative Adversarial Network (SRGAN) and Tikhonov Regularization-Based Restoration to reduce noise and improve image quality.
Hair Removal: Use of the 'dullrazor' function involving grayscale conversion, black hat morphological operation, Gaussian blur, and binary thresholding to remove hair and dark artifacts.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to generate additional training samples by applying various transformations including rotations, flips, and color shifts. This approach was investigated for its impact on data leakage, as it led to inflated accuracy (88%) but inconsistent metrics for minority classes compared to the non-augmented model (77.42%).
The primary model was trained without data augmentation to prevent data leakage. The dataset was balanced by standardizing the number of images per stage to 40.

conclusions: The study establishes the viability of using a few-shot learning approach with Siamese networks and ResNet encoders to classify melanoma stages based on lesion thickness from dermoscopic images, addressing data scarcity challenges.

The proposed Siamese network model achieved a promising accuracy of 77% on a limited, balanced dataset (40 images per stage) without data augmentation, demonstrating its potential for medical image analysis with limited labeled data.

Rigorous data handling practices, specifically ensuring the independence of training and validation sets and avoiding data augmentation techniques, are critical to prevent data leakage and ensure realistic performance assessment.

While a model trained with ImageDataGenerator augmentation achieved higher accuracy (88%), it exhibited inconsistencies in evaluation metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) for minority classes, highlighting the risk of inflated performance estimates due to data leakage.

The findings underscore the importance of avoiding data augmentation in few-shot medical image analysis tasks to maintain the reliability of performance metrics, particularly for classes with fewer samples.

The non-invasive nature of the proposed model offers a potential alternative to traditional staging methods, which could aid clinicians in treatment planning and improve patient outcomes by reducing the need for invasive biopsies.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset (40 images per stage, 120 total) curated from the ISIC Archive, limiting statistical power and generalizability.

The paper explicitly highlights data leakage risks associated with data augmentation; the augmented model achieved 88% accuracy but showed inconsistent metrics for minority classes (Stage II support dropped to 6), suggesting inflated performance estimates.
The primary model achieved 77.42% accuracy on a test set of only 31 samples (90/5/5 split), which is a critically small sample size for reliable evaluation.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the curated ISIC subset, raising concerns about real-world clinical applicability.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance tests (p-values) or confidence intervals for the reported accuracy and classification metrics.
The dataset was artificially balanced by standardizing to 40 images per stage to prevent leakage, which may not reflect the true class imbalance distribution in clinical practice.

The ResNet-18 encoder was pre-trained on ImageNet (general object classes), not specifically on medical or dermatological data, which may limit feature extraction efficacy for subtle lesion thickness differences.
The study is a conference paper with limited scope; the authors acknowledge that further improvements and expansion to include wider skin types are necessary for clinical integration.
